items.q: Was the October Revolution a huge popular battle?
items.correct: No - it was a planned seizure of key points by relatively small Bolshevik forces, and the Winter Palace fell with little fighting, though the Bolsheviks had real support in the soviets.
items.misconception: Thousands died storming the Winter Palace in a massive battle.
items.random_wrong: The October Revolution took place mainly in Moscow.
items.why: Later propaganda films dramatised the storming; students repeat the epic version instead of the organised coup the evidence shows.

items.q: Did Lenin choose Stalin as his successor?
items.correct: No - Lenin's Testament criticised Stalin and suggested removing him as General Secretary; Stalin outmanoeuvred rivals like Trotsky in the power struggle of 1924-29.
items.misconception: Stalin was Lenin's chosen and announced heir.
items.random_wrong: Stalin was elected leader by a national vote of the Soviet people.
items.why: Stalin's propaganda painted him as Lenin's faithful pupil, so students miss the suppressed Testament and the contested succession.

items.q: Did collectivisation quickly increase food production?
items.correct: No - peasants slaughtered livestock in protest, output fell, and state grain seizures contributed to the devastating famine of 1932-33, especially in Ukraine.
items.misconception: Collectivisation immediately modernised farming and ended hunger.
items.random_wrong: Collectivisation gave each peasant family its own private farm.
items.why: Students assume a modernising policy must have worked; the human cost and production collapse are central to evaluating Stalin's rule.

items.q: Who organised and led the Red Army during the Civil War?
items.why: Correct answer: Trotsky. “Stalin” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Why did Lenin introduce the New Economic Policy in 1921?
items.correct: To revive the economy after War Communism had caused famine and revolts such as Kronstadt
items.misconception: To prepare Russia for war with Germany
items.random_wrong: To hand the economy over to foreign companies
items.why: It's To revive the economy after War Communism had caused famine and revolts such as Kronstadt. “To prepare Russia for war with Germany” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.random_wrong: Factories built under the Five-Year Plans
items.why: It's Collective farms created under Stalin. “Secret police units” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What happened at the Moscow show trials of 1936-38?
items.correct: Leading Old Bolsheviks confessed to fabricated crimes and most were executed
items.misconception: Stalin himself was put on trial by the party
items.random_wrong: Tsarist generals were tried for the First World War
items.why: It's Leading Old Bolsheviks confessed to fabricated crimes and most were executed. “Stalin himself was put on trial by the party” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did the Bolsheviks seize power?
items.why: The real figure is 1917 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
